params.text:
Putting people in control of their own lives

The Mental Capacity Act 2005 (MCA) is the law that protects and empowers people aged 16 and over who may not be able to make some decisions for themselves. Every day, adults you support will make choices — what to eat, whether to accept care, how to spend their money, which treatment to agree to. The MCA exists to make sure those choices stay with the person wherever possible, and that when someone genuinely cannot decide, the right people step in lawfully and in the person’s best interests.

The Act is backed by a statutory Code of Practice. As a care worker you have a legal duty to ‘have regard to’ the Code — in other words, you must follow it unless you have a good reason not to. Getting this wrong can mean a person’s rights are breached, and can leave you and your employer acting unlawfully.

  • The MCA applies to a huge range of decisions — from everyday care to serious medical treatment.
  • It covers people whose capacity may be affected by dementia, a learning disability, a brain injury, mental illness, a stroke, or even temporary states such as intoxication or unconsciousness.
  • It is designed to support decision-making, not to take decisions away.

params.alt: The five principles of the Mental Capacity Act

params.text:
Capacity is not all-or-nothing

A common myth is that a person either ‘has capacity’ or ‘doesn’t’. The MCA rejects this. Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific.

  • Decision-specific: someone may lack the capacity to decide where to live but be perfectly able to choose what to wear or what to eat. You must never assume that struggling with one decision means a person cannot make any decisions.
  • Time-specific: capacity can fluctuate. A person may be able to decide in the morning but not in the evening, or their capacity may improve as an infection is treated. Assess capacity at the moment the decision needs to be made.

You must also never judge capacity on the basis of a person’s age, appearance, diagnosis, or behaviour that others find unusual.

params.question: A person who lacks the capacity to manage their finances automatically lacks the capacity to decide what to wear each day.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Capacity is decision-specific — it must be assessed separately for each decision, and a person can lack capacity for one while retaining it for another.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Capacity is decision-specific. Lacking capacity for one decision tells you nothing about another, simpler decision.
